title: Spinning silk hankies
subtitle: Drafting Mawata silk hankies on a wheel or drop spindle for a lustrous, strong singles
excerpt: Silk hankies are square layers of degummed silk cocoon stretched flat during processing. Each layer is a single cocoon, and a stack of 10-20 layers makes one hankie. The silk is drafted directly from the hankie by stretching each layer into a long, filament-based singles of extraordinary strength and sheen.
sourceNotes: Silk hankie spinning is documented in UK and Australian spinning guild instruction and contemporary fibre arts education. The Mawata (Japanese: stretched silk) process is the standard preparation for hankie spinning.

glossaryTerms.definition: Squares of silk fibre made by stretching a single degummed silk cocoon over a small frame while still hot and wet, then drying flat. A finished silk hankie is a translucent square of aligned silk filaments that can be pulled apart into a fine, lustrous sheet for spinning or crafting.

Silk in its continuous, unbroken fibre form as produced by the silkworm. Filament silk (as used in silk hankies) differs from spun silk (silk noil, silk top) in that the fibre length is theoretically the full length of the cocoon thread. Filament silk is stronger and more lustrous than spun silk.

attrs.title: Spinning wheel safety
attrs.body: Tie back hair and keep loose clothing away from the drive band and flyer when the wheel is running.

content.text: spin differently from any other fibre because the silk is in

form rather than staple form. There is no staple length to work with, no crimp to give elasticity, and no scales to grab each other. Twist alone holds the yarn together. Silk therefore needs more twist than wool of equivalent weight to hold during knitting or weaving.

items.name: Spinning wheel with a small orifice and high-ratio whorl

content.text: Step 1: Separate and pre-draft the hankie

Peel one layer off the hankie stack. Hold it at two opposite corners and stretch slowly until it begins to thin into a sheet. Keep stretching, walking your hands along the sheet as it lengthens, until the layer has been pulled into a long band of thin, aligned filaments. This pre-drafting step determines how fine or thick the finished yarn will be: more stretching produces a finer yarn.
Step 2: Attach to the leader and begin spinning

Use a high-ratio whorl and a fast treadle speed: silk needs twist at roughly twice the rate you would use for an equivalent-weight merino. Overlap the end of the silk band with the leader yarn and treadle to add twist to the join. Draft from the silk band by pulling slowly back with the back hand while the front hand controls twist entry. Silk drafts differently from wool: there is very little resistance and no springback, so the draft motion must be even and controlled.

When one layer is nearly spun, overlap the end of the next pre-drafted layer with the last few centimetres of the current one. Treadle to twist them together. The join in silk singles is less forgiving than in wool: keep the overlap to at least 4-5 cm and let the twist run through the overlap fully before drafting further.

Silk singles are very strong and can be used as laceweight singles without plying. For a 2-ply yarn, ply using S-twist at a ratio of two S-treadles for every three Z-treadles used in the singles. Wind into a skein on the niddy-noddy. Silk does not need setting by snapping: soak in cool water for 10 minutes, squeeze gently in a towel, and dry. The finished silk yarn requires no blocking and retains its sheen indefinitely.

items.problem: The singles break repeatedly at the same point
items.cause: Under-twist in the singles; silk needs more twist than most fibres
items.fix: Increase the wheel ratio or slow down the drafting speed while keeping treadle speed the same. More treadles per metre of yarn adds twist.

items.problem: The yarn is much thicker in some sections than others
items.cause: The hankie layer was not pre-drafted to an even thickness before spinning
items.fix: Spend more time in the pre-drafting step, stretching each layer to a consistent thickness before attaching it to the leader.
